learning_outcomes.description: Express an awareness and understanding of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander identities, histories and cultures.
Understand the challenges facing Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples in the health and/or education sector.
Work responsively and effectively with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples in the health and/or education sectors.
Work effectively within a student team to develop communication and process management skills, and facilitate peer-assisted collaborative learning

assessments.task_description:
Active participation in all scheduled workshop classes is required in Weeks 1, 2, 4, 8 and 13. Students will conduct research and/or develop other relevant information during each workshop and will be expected to analyse, interpret and synthesise this material in line with the specific requirements discussed in class. Through these activities, students will demonstrate practical research skills, data synthesis, and the ability to engage with research literature.

During each workshop, students will conduct research relevant to the specific learning module. Basic guidance for data collection and analysis will be provided. Students will then examine the data in relation to a research question developed in Week 1 Workshop. This will require students to create appropriate visualisations or tables to present their findings to the class.

Students will also be required to consult relevant research literature and relate their findings to existing knowledge in the field. This process will involve critical analysis of the literature to demonstrate an understanding of the methods used in the workshop, as well as the relevance of the research question or goal to the broader field.


Use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) 

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Task to be completed during designated workshop and submitted through Learn.UQ assessment link.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Written Research Poster Component:

You are required to complete a research poster. The research poster component must is limited to one-page document sized for A0 reproduction (including references). The referencing style used must be Vancouver. Font style and line spacing is also at the discretion of the student and readability and ease of interpretation is important. Content of the research poster must include sections on Introduction, Methods, Results and Conclusion as well as References. 

 

Students will decide on their topic in Week 1 Workshop of semester.

Broad focus areas for your Poster and associated research project ( it is recommended that you choose one from the list):

  1. What are First Nations peoples’ experiences of receiving exercise physiology support for type 2 diabetes, and what factors influence cultural safety, trust, and engagement?
  2. How do First Nations peoples perceive dietetic advice for type 2 diabetes, and what makes nutrition support feel culturally relevant, practical, and respectful?
  3. What are First Nations peoples’ experiences of cardiac rehabilitation, and what barriers or enablers shape participation and completion?
  4. How do First Nations peoples understand the role of physical activity in heart health, and what supports or limits participation?
  5. How do First Nations families perceive health promotion messages about childhood weight, nutrition, and physical activity?
  6. How do First Nations students experience HPE classes, and what makes these lessons feel inclusive, culturally safe, or disconnected from their lives?
  7. How do First Nations students perceive the inclusion of traditional games in HPE, and how does this influence cultural identity, belonging, and participation?
  8. How do First Nations athletes experience strength and conditioning programs, and what practices support culturally safe athlete development?
  9. How do First Nations young people describe the role of sport in connection, confidence, identity, belonging, and wellbeing?
  10. How do First Nations men experience community sport programs, and what role do these programs play in health, connection, leadership, and social support?
  11. What are First Nations peoples’ experiences of exercise-based rehabilitation for musculoskeletal pain or injury?
  12. How do First Nations peoples understand the relationship between movement, Country, community, culture, and social and emotional wellbeing?
  13. How do First Nations peoples experience food access, affordability, and nutrition advice, and what makes healthy eating recommendations realistic or unrealistic
  14. How do First Nations parents and caregivers understand children’s movement, confidence, cultural connection, and lifelong engagement in physical activity?
  15. What are First Nations peoples’ experiences of receiving exercise, nutrition, or allied health support for metabolic associated fatty liver disease?

 

Please note:

Students must select a research topic that directly relates to their chosen profession or future area of professional practice. The topic should not be a general Indigenous Health issue unless it is clearly connected to the student’s disciplinary pathway, such as sport and exercise science, clinical exercise physiology, health and physical education teaching, nutrition and dietetics, coaching, sport development, public health, or another relevant health-related profession.

 

The purpose of this requirement is to ensure that students can demonstrate how Indigenous Health knowledge applies to their future professional role. Students should therefore choose a topic that allows them to examine the experiences, perceptions, needs, priorities, or perspectives of First Nations peoples in relation to the work they may undertake as future practitioners, educators, coaches, researchers, or health professionals. For example, a student studying exercise physiology may examine First Nations peoples’ experiences of exercise physiology services for type 2 diabetes management, while a student studying health and physical education may examine First Nations students’ experiences of culturally responsive HPE teaching. A nutrition and dietetics student may examine First Nations peoples’ perceptions of dietetic advice for chronic disease management or food security.

 

Students may examine their topic within the broader CANZAUS First Nations context or narrow their focus specifically to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples in Australia. In either case, the selected topic must have a clear connection to the student’s chosen profession and allow them to communicate relevant background, methodology, results, conclusions, practical applications, and quality references in their research poster.

 

 

CANZAUS First Nations refers to Indigenous peoples from the settler-colonial nations of Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and the United States. In this context, the term recognises the shared, but distinct, histories and contemporary experiences of First Nations peoples across these countries, including experiences of colonisation, dispossession, cultural continuity, health inequities, self-determination, and community-led approaches to wellbeing.

 

For this research project, students may choose to examine Indigenous Health within the broader CANZAUS First Nations context, drawing on literature from Indigenous populations across Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and/or the United States. Alternatively, students may narrow their project to focus specifically on Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples in Australia. A more specific Australian focus may be particularly useful where students want to examine local health systems, professional practice, policy, education, sport, physical activity, nutrition, or culturally responsive approaches within the Australian context.

 

Background to the Task

Students are required to complete a systematized literature review as the research method for their project. This means students will investigate their chosen Indigenous Health topic by identifying, selecting, summarising, and synthesising existing peer-reviewed literature in a structured and transparent way. The review must directly relate to the student’s chosen profession or future professional practice and should focus on the experiences, perceptions, priorities, or perspectives of First Nations peoples.

 

A systematized literature review is appropriate for this assessment because it allows students to engage with research evidence in a rigorous but manageable way. Students are expected to develop a clear research question, search selected academic databases using relevant keywords, apply inclusion and exclusion criteria, screen the literature, summarise key studies, and present the major findings or themes in their research poster. This aligns with the poster requirements to include background, methods, results, conclusions, practical applications, and quality references.

 

Difference between a systematized review, systematic review, and scoping review

A systematized review includes several features of a systematic review, such as a clear research question, planned search strategy, inclusion and exclusion criteria, and a structured approach to summarising the literature. However, it is usually smaller in scale and does not need to meet all the methodological requirements of a full systematic review. For this assessment, students are not expected to complete exhaustive searching across all relevant databases, register a protocol, conduct duplicate screening, or complete a full risk-of-bias assessment unless specifically directed.

 

A systematic review is a highly rigorous and comprehensive form of literature review. It aims to identify, appraise, and synthesise all available evidence on a specific research question using pre-defined methods. Systematic reviews often include protocol registration, extensive database searching, duplicate screening by more than one reviewer, critical appraisal of study quality, and, where appropriate, meta-analysis. This level of review is generally beyond the scope of this undergraduate assessment.

 

A scoping review is used to map the breadth and nature of literature on a broader topic. Rather than answering a tightly focused question about effectiveness or experience, a scoping review explores what research exists, identifies key concepts, clarifies definitions, and highlights gaps in the literature. Scoping reviews are particularly useful when a topic is emerging, complex, or has not yet been comprehensively reviewed.

 

Simple comparison

Students will be required to complete their systematized literature review using one academic database only. This restriction is intentional and is designed to keep the project manageable within the scope of the assessment while still requiring students to demonstrate a structured and transparent approach to searching, screening, and synthesising research literature. Students should select one appropriate database relevant to their topic and discipline, such as Scopus, CINAHL, PubMed, SPORTDiscus, Web of Science, ERIC, or PsycINFO. The selected database should be clearly identified in the Methods section of the research poster. Students must also report their search terms, inclusion and exclusion criteria, and the process used to identify the final sources included in their review.

 

Although full systematic and scoping reviews usually require searching multiple databases, this assessment is designed as a systematized literature review. Therefore, students are not expected to conduct an exhaustive search of all available evidence. Instead, they are expected to demonstrate that they can apply key features of a rigorous review process in a focused and achievable way. This includes developing a clear research question, searching one relevant database, screening literature systematically, summarising the evidence, and presenting findings clearly through their research poster.

 

Students must use Covidence to manage the article screening stage of their systematised review. Once students have completed their search using their selected academic database, they are required to export the search results and upload them into Covidence. Students will then screen articles by title and abstract using their pre-determined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Articles that appear eligible will progress to full-text screening, where final decisions about inclusion in the review will be made. The screening process must be clearly reported in the Methods section of the research poster. This should include the number of articles identified through the database search, the number screened, the number excluded, and the number included in the final review. Access to Covidence is available through the UQ Library website and is free for UQ students through the University of Queensland institutional licence. Students should use their UQ username or email address to request access through the UQ Library Covidence page before beginning their review.

 

 Students must use the PCC Framework to develop and refine their research question for the systematised literature review. The PCC Framework includes Population, Concept, and Context, and is commonly used to structure review questions, particularly when exploring experiences, perceptions, priorities, or perspectives. For this assessment, students must clearly identify each component of their PCC Framework before commencing their database search:

  • Population refers to the group of people the review is focused on. In this assessment, the population must relate to CANZAUS First Nations peoples or, more specifically, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.
  • Concept refers to the main issue, phenomenon, experience, or professional practice area being examined. This may include topics such as physical activity participation, exercise physiology services, nutrition and dietetic care, sport participation, Health and Physical Education teaching, rehabilitation, chronic disease management, or social and emotional wellbeing.
  • Context refers to the setting, system, condition, or professional environment in which the topic is being examined. This may include schools, community sport, primary health care, allied health services, remote communities, urban settings, chronic disease programs, or education environments.

 

Students must use the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Quality Appraisal Tool (QAT) as part of the quality appraisal process for their systematised literature review. This tool is designed to support appraisal of research quality from an Indigenous perspective and asks students to consider whether the research demonstrates appropriate engagement with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, priorities, governance, ethical practice, cultural responsiveness, and benefit. After students have completed full-text screening and selected the final articles for inclusion in their review, they must apply the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Quality Appraisal Tool to each included article. Students should use the tool to assess the quality, relevance, and cultural integrity of the research, rather than only assessing conventional methodological quality. This process will help students consider whether the included studies were conducted in ways that are respectful, ethical, community-informed, and meaningful for First Nations peoples.

 

Students must report the quality appraisal process in the Methods section of their research poster. In the Results section, students should briefly summarise the outcomes of the appraisal, including any key strengths or limitations identified across the included studies. The appraisal should also inform the Conclusion and Practical Applications sections by helping students reflect on the quality of the evidence and what this means for their chosen profession or future professional practice.

 

Where students are reviewing literature from the broader CANZAUS First Nations context, they should still use the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Quality Appraisal Tool to guide their thinking about Indigenous research quality. However, students should acknowledge that the tool was developed specifically for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health research and should be applied thoughtfully when considering First Nations research from Canada, Aotearoa New Zealand, or the United States.

 

Use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) 

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.task_description:
You will be required to give a brief presentation of your research project, using the 3-minute presentation format.

What is the Three Minute Thesis (3MT)?

It is an activity in developing academic and research communication skills. Students have 3-minutes to present a compelling oration on their research and its significance in language appropriate to an educated but non-specialist audience. It is not an activity in trivialising or to 'dumb-down' research. The oration should engage the audience without reducing research to entertainment value alone.


Further information for your presentation is as follows:

• You are limited to a ONE slide (e.g. PowerPoint or Keynote equivalent with no animation).

• Presenters have a time limit of 3 minutes.

• No additional electronic media (e.g. sound and video files) are permitted beyond the voice over recording.

• No additional props (e.g. costumes, lab/musical instruments etc.) are allowed.


This assessment task is to be completed through a recorded presentation and submitted through the online Learn.UQ assignment submission link.

 

Students will decide on their topic in Week 1 Workshop of semester and it will be the same as the topic for their Research Poster presentation.


Presentations must clearly explain how the research contributes to understanding diversity within and across First Nations peoples, including differences in culture, perspectives, languages, histories, and community contexts. Students must also demonstrate the value of engaging with First Nations communities to co-design meaningful and successful outcomes in culturally safe ways.

 

This 3MT must be directly connected to the student’s chosen profession. For example, students may consider how their research informs the embedding of First Nations ways of knowing, being, and doing in the HPE curriculum, culturally responsive practice in nutrition and dietetics, or culturally safe exercise prescription for First Nations peoples.


The ability to communicate research findings clearly, confidently, and persuasively is a fundamental component of innovative and impactful research practice. In this assessment, students will demonstrate their capacity to translate complex research into a concise and engaging three-minute presentation format. This task requires students to communicate the significance, methods, key findings, and implications of their research in a manner that is accessible, professional, and scientifically informed.

 

Students will also demonstrate their ability to use contemporary communication technologies to prepare and share their presentation for their video. This includes recording the presentation using screen capture, uploading the final presentation to Learn.UQ, and submitting the presentation for assessment by the course team. This assessment represents the culmination of the research undertaken in this course. It provides students with an opportunity to showcase their ability to communicate research verbally in a professional scientific format, while also demonstrating the broader communication skills required to translate research knowledge for diverse audiences.

 

Use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Three-minute presentation to be submitted via the relevant Turnitin submission portal on the course Blackboard site.

assessments.task_description:
The Final Examination will give you the chance to showcase everything you’ve learned across the semester.

You’ll have 120 minutes to complete 60 multiple-choice questions and 1 extended answer question all designed to assess your understanding of key concepts, critical thinking, and application of theory to practice.

The exam will draw on reading material covered from Weeks 1 to 13 available through Learn.UQ. This is your opportunity to demonstrate your growth, insight, and readiness to apply your knowledge in real-world professional contexts.

Use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) 

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
EMPOWERING RESEARCH IN FIRST NATIONS STUDIES

In Week 1, there is a scheduled face-to-face workshop giving students the opportunity to fully immerse themselves in the content provided and requirements of the course. The content explores key themes such as identity, sovereignty, and social identity theory, which lay the foundation for culturally responsive practice. Students are expected to engage actively with the readings and learning materials during this week to prepare for discussions and assessments. Starting strong in Week 1 will help you build a deeper understanding of the course and give you a solid base for the semester ahead—so dive in and come prepared to connect your learning to practice!


Learning outcomes: L01, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: EMPOWERING RESEARCH IN FIRST NATIONS STUDIES In Week 1, there is a scheduled face-to-face workshop giving students the opportunity to fully immerse themselves in the content provided and requirements of the course. The content explores key themes such as identity, sovereignty, and social identity theory, which lay the foundation for culturally responsive practice. Students are expected to engage actively with the readings and learning materials during this week to prepare for discussions and assessments. Starting strong in Week 1 will help you build a deeper understanding of the course and give you a solid base for the semester ahead—so dive in and come prepared to connect your learning to practice!

learning_activities.topic:
FIRST NATIONS HISTORY AND ESTABLISHING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH

This week explores the historical foundations that continue to shape the lived experiences of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples today. Key lecture topics include invasion, protection, assimilation, and the ongoing impacts of colonisation on health, identity, and education. Through engaging with these themes, students will begin to unpack how history informs contemporary practice and professional responsibilities.


Learning outcomes: L01, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: FIRST NATIONS HISTORY AND ESTABLISHING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH
learning_activities.topic_description: FIRST NATIONS HISTORY AND ESTABLISHING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH This week explores the historical foundations that continue to shape the lived experiences of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples today. Key lecture topics include invasion, protection, assimilation, and the ongoing impacts of colonisation on health, identity, and education. Through engaging with these themes, students will begin to unpack how history informs contemporary practice and professional responsibilities.

learning_activities.topic:
DATA ANALYSIS IN FIRST NATIONS STUDIES AND BEHAVIOUR CHANGE

This week introduces key frameworks for understanding behaviour change in the context of working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Students will explore models such as Bronfenbrenner’s ecological systems theory, Ring Theory of Personhood, and culturally grounded approaches to First Nations behaviour change. The concept of locating oneself—understanding one’s own values, cultural background, and position—is central to the week's learning and sets the foundation for reflexive and relational practice.


Learning outcomes: L02
learning_activities.topic_title: DATA ANALYSIS IN FIRST NATIONS STUDIES AND BEHAVIOUR CHANGE
learning_activities.topic_description: DATA ANALYSIS IN FIRST NATIONS STUDIES AND BEHAVIOUR CHANGE This week introduces key frameworks for understanding behaviour change in the context of working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Students will explore models such as Bronfenbrenner’s ecological systems theory, Ring Theory of Personhood, and culturally grounded approaches to First Nations behaviour change. The concept of locating oneself—understanding one’s own values, cultural background, and position—is central to the week's learning and sets the foundation for reflexive and relational practice.

learning_activities.topic:
NAVIGATING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND REFLEXIVITY

This week centres on the critical skill of reflexivity—the ability to examine your own beliefs, values, and assumptions in the context of your professional practice. Students will be introduced to reflexivity theory, the role of self in culturally responsive work, and the importance of forming values-based partnerships with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. The concept of deep looking is introduced as a way of developing greater self-awareness and relational accountability.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: NAVIGATING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND REFLEXIVITY This week centres on the critical skill of reflexivity—the ability to examine your own beliefs, values, and assumptions in the context of your professional practice. Students will be introduced to reflexivity theory, the role of self in culturally responsive work, and the importance of forming values-based partnerships with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. The concept of deep looking is introduced as a way of developing greater self-awareness and relational accountability.

learning_activities.topic:
FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH FRAMEWORKS AND TRAUMA-INFORMED PRACTICE

This week introduces students to trauma-informed and neuro-informed approaches when working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Drawing on national and international frameworks students will explore the theories that underpin culturally safe engagement in contexts where intergenerational trauma, systemic disadvantage, and resilience intersect. Emphasis is placed on understanding trauma not as an individual deficit, but within broader social, cultural, and historical contexts.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_title: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH FRAMEWORKS AND TRAUMA-INFORMED PRACTICE
learning_activities.topic_description: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH FRAMEWORKS AND TRAUMA-INFORMED PRACTICE This week introduces students to trauma-informed and neuro-informed approaches when working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Drawing on national and international frameworks students will explore the theories that underpin culturally safe engagement in contexts where intergenerational trauma, systemic disadvantage, and resilience intersect. Emphasis is placed on understanding trauma not as an individual deficit, but within broader social, cultural, and historical contexts.

learning_activities.topic:
FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH ANALYSIS AND CULTURALLY RESPONSIVE PRACTICE

This week focuses on developing a strong foundation in culturally responsive practice, exploring the principles of cultural safety, models of engagement, and the transformative power of Deep Listening (Dadirri). Students will examine how culturally responsive approaches are co-created with communities, and how respectful, relational practice can foster trust, collaboration, and meaningful outcomes for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH ANALYSIS AND CULTURALLY RESPONSIVE PRACTICE
learning_activities.topic_description: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH ANALYSIS AND CULTURALLY RESPONSIVE PRACTICE This week focuses on developing a strong foundation in culturally responsive practice, exploring the principles of cultural safety, models of engagement, and the transformative power of Deep Listening (Dadirri). Students will examine how culturally responsive approaches are co-created with communities, and how respectful, relational practice can foster trust, collaboration, and meaningful outcomes for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.

learning_activities.topic:
FIRST NATIONS QUALITY APPRAISALS IN RESEARCH AND RIGHTS BASED HEALTH

This week explores the significance of rights-based frameworks in advancing health equity for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Students will examine concepts of self-determination, human rights, and community-controlled health, alongside key instruments such as the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP). The week highlights how embedding rights into practice supports justice, empowerment, and long-term wellbeing.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: FIRST NATIONS QUALITY APPRAISALS IN RESEARCH AND RIGHTS BASED HEALTH
learning_activities.topic_description: FIRST NATIONS QUALITY APPRAISALS IN RESEARCH AND RIGHTS BASED HEALTH This week explores the significance of rights-based frameworks in advancing health equity for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Students will examine concepts of self-determination, human rights, and community-controlled health, alongside key instruments such as the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP). The week highlights how embedding rights into practice supports justice, empowerment, and long-term wellbeing.

learning_activities.topic:
FINDINGS FROM FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND ETHICS OF ENGAGEMENT

This week focuses on the ethical foundations of working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and communities. Students will explore the AIATSIS Code of Ethics and principles of culturally responsive ethics in practice. The week highlights the importance of ethical engagement that is grounded in respect, reciprocity, accountability, and Indigenous leadership in research, health, and education settings.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: FINDINGS FROM FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND ETHICS OF ENGAGEMENT
learning_activities.topic_description: FINDINGS FROM FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND ETHICS OF ENGAGEMENT This week focuses on the ethical foundations of working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and communities. Students will explore the AIATSIS Code of Ethics and principles of culturally responsive ethics in practice. The week highlights the importance of ethical engagement that is grounded in respect, reciprocity, accountability, and Indigenous leadership in research, health, and education settings.

learning_activities.topic:
FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH VISUALISATION AND THE RECONCILIATION AGENDA

This week delves into the national Reconciliation agenda, exploring its relationship to social justice, truth-telling, and the role of Reconciliation Action Plans (RAPs) across institutions and sectors. Students will engage with the importance of historical truth, acknowledgment, and reparative action in creating a more equitable future. The concept of yarning is also introduced as a culturally grounded method of communication that supports relationship-building and shared understanding.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH VISUALISATION AND THE RECONCILIATION AGENDA
learning_activities.topic_description: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH VISUALISATION AND THE RECONCILIATION AGENDA This week delves into the national Reconciliation agenda, exploring its relationship to social justice, truth-telling, and the role of Reconciliation Action Plans (RAPs) across institutions and sectors. Students will engage with the importance of historical truth, acknowledgment, and reparative action in creating a more equitable future. The concept of yarning is also introduced as a culturally grounded method of communication that supports relationship-building and shared understanding.

learning_activities.topic:
PRESENTING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH IN FIRST NATIONS POLICY

This week explores the role of policy in shaping outcomes for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, with a particular focus on Closing the Gap targets, the intersection of sport and policy, and how national strategies influence professional practice. Students will critically examine the effectiveness of current approaches and reflect on how policy frameworks can support self-determination and culturally safe service delivery.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: PRESENTING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH IN FIRST NATIONS POLICY
learning_activities.topic_description: PRESENTING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH IN FIRST NATIONS POLICY This week explores the role of policy in shaping outcomes for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, with a particular focus on Closing the Gap targets, the intersection of sport and policy, and how national strategies influence professional practice. Students will critically examine the effectiveness of current approaches and reflect on how policy frameworks can support self-determination and culturally safe service delivery.

learning_activities.topic:
PRESENTING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND THE DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH

This week focuses on the social, cultural, and structural determinants of health that shape the wellbeing of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Students will explore how factors such as housing, education, cultural identity, racism, and connection to Country influence health outcomes. The week highlights the need for strengths-based, culturally grounded approaches that address systemic inequities while recognising the resilience and knowledge within communities.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: PRESENTING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND THE DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH
learning_activities.topic_description: PRESENTING FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND THE DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH This week focuses on the social, cultural, and structural determinants of health that shape the wellbeing of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Students will explore how factors such as housing, education, cultural identity, racism, and connection to Country influence health outcomes. The week highlights the need for strengths-based, culturally grounded approaches that address systemic inequities while recognising the resilience and knowledge within communities.

learning_activities.topic:
OUTPUTS FROM FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND SOCIAL-EMOTIONAL WELLBEING

This week explores the concept of Social and Emotional Wellbeing (SEWB), a holistic framework grounded in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander worldviews. Students will examine the principles of SEWB, its cultural and community foundations, and how it differs from Western models of mental health. Emphasis will be placed on how SEWB influences professional practice across health, education, sport, and community sectors, and how it can be supported through culturally informed strategies and environments.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: OUTPUTS FROM FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND SOCIAL-EMOTIONAL WELLBEING
learning_activities.topic_description: OUTPUTS FROM FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH AND SOCIAL-EMOTIONAL WELLBEING This week explores the concept of Social and Emotional Wellbeing (SEWB), a holistic framework grounded in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander worldviews. Students will examine the principles of SEWB, its cultural and community foundations, and how it differs from Western models of mental health. Emphasis will be placed on how SEWB influences professional practice across health, education, sport, and community sectors, and how it can be supported through culturally informed strategies and environments.

learning_activities.topic:
FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH TRANSLATION AND DATA SOVEREIGNTY

In the final week of the course, students will explore the critical importance of Indigenous data sovereignty and the governance of data relating to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Topics include the Mayi Kuwayu Study, the CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance, and how ethical, strengths-based approaches to data collection and use support self-determination, community control, and cultural integrity. Students will reflect on how data practices can uphold or undermine equity and justice in their professional fields.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH TRANSLATION AND DATA SOVEREIGNTY
learning_activities.topic_description: FIRST NATIONS RESEARCH TRANSLATION AND DATA SOVEREIGNTY In the final week of the course, students will explore the critical importance of Indigenous data sovereignty and the governance of data relating to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. Topics include the Mayi Kuwayu Study, the CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance, and how ethical, strengths-based approaches to data collection and use support self-determination, community control, and cultural integrity. Students will reflect on how data practices can uphold or undermine equity and justice in their professional fields.
